title: Circuit Gilles Villeneuve
sub: A stop-and-go island circuit on the St. Lawrence: long straights, brutal braking into tight chicanes, a famous hairpin, and the unforgiving Wall of Champions on the way home.
mapCredit: Will Pittenger / Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA

sectors.name: Sector 1
sectors.char: Short straight into the Turn 1-2 chicane, then flowing esses and chicanes along the river.

sectors.name: Sector 2
sectors.char: Quick chicanes, a DRS straight and the heavy stop into the Turn 10 hairpin.

sectors.name: Sector 3
sectors.char: The long back straight, top DRS zone, into the final chicane and the Wall of Champions.

tyreAlloc: Softest in the range · C3 / C4 / C5
stratNote: Low grip early and a smooth surface keep wear modest. The hard braking causes graining more than thermal deg, so warm-up and front-locking matter.
spots: End of the back straight + DRS, the longest zone. The big braking move into the Wall.
Slowest corner on the lap; out-brake on entry, switchback on exit.
Off the short main straight + DRS, a tight right-left for early moves.
otNote: Three DRS zones, big braking, and low grip make for plentiful overtaking. The walls punish errors: the Wall of Champions has bitten title winners.
corners: Tight opening chicane off the short main straight; first-lap flashpoint.
Quick chicane keeping the rhythm along the river.
Short left linking the opening complexes.
Flowing esses that flick the car between the walls.
Quick chicane before the run to the hairpin.
The slowest corner; tight 180° right and a classic overtaking spot.
Opens onto the long back straight and top speeds.
Slight kink at the end of the back straight before braking.
Heavy braking into the last chicane; the Wall of Champions on the exit.
geo: Built on Île Notre-Dame, a man-made island in the St. Lawrence created for Expo 67. Pancake-flat, ringed by water and walls.
The exit wall at the final chicane, named after 1999 when Hill, Hakkinen and Villeneuve all hit it. Still claims victims.
Long full-throttle straights into slow chicanes and the hairpin. Hard on brakes and traction; low downforce.
Tight walls and low grip mean Montreal almost always throws a safety car. Strategy is built around when.
